narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've already shown incredible determination by pursuing this path despite the unique challenges many face, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands above the typical student's 3.21. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 46% chance of failure—high enough that your application was rejected to align with our goal of selecting students most likely to succeed. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, which had the strongest negative influence on the model's view of your bar exam readiness. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA was the top factor pulling in the opposite direction, strongly suggesting you'd pass by demonstrating consistent academic strength from your undergrad days. Your LSAT score of 33, though below the average 36.7, also helped nudge the prediction toward success, showing it recognizes your potential despite not being in the top range. Lesser influences included your Year 1 decile of 3, slightly under the average 5.7 and tilting toward failure risk, while your middle family income bracket and full-time student status both mildly supported a passing outcome, in line with the majority of students who are full-time. Compared to typical applicants, your profile shines in undergrad performance but lags in later school deciles and LSAT, which together outweighed the positives for this model. This isn't a judgment on your worth or ability—many successful lawyers overcome similar hurdles through targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your high school decile performance in any remaining years if applicable, and gathering letters highlighting your undergrad success and resilience as a Black woman in this field. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or bar prep resources tailored to diverse backgrounds. We're rooting for you—persistence like yours changes outcomes every day. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
